--- SOURCE 1: Linguistic landscape - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linguistic_landscape

SUMMARY:
The linguistic landscape refers to the visibility and salience of languages on public and commercial signs in a given territory or region.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Linguistic landscape

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Seattle_trash_lese_rac_basura_200511.jpg/250px-Seattle_trash_lese_rac_basura_200511.jpg)

The **linguistic landscape** refers to the "visibility and salience of languages on public and commercial [signs](/wiki/Signage "Signage") in a given territory or region".[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Linguistic landscape research has been described as being "somewhere at the junction of sociolinguistics, sociology, social psychology, geography, and media studies".[[2]](#cite_note-2)
It is a concept which originated in [sociolinguistics](/wiki/Sociolinguistics "Sociolinguistics") and [language policy](/wiki/Language_policy "Language policy") as scholars studied how languages are visually displayed and hierarchised in [multilingual](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism") societies, from large metropolitan centers to Amazonia.[[3]](#cite_note-3) For example, linguistic landscape scholars have described how and why some public signs in Jerusalem are presented in Hebrew, English, and Arabic, or a combination thereof.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It also looks at how communication in public space plays a crucial role in the organisation of society.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Development of the field of study

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Carreg_Fedd_Ddwyieithog_-_Bilingual_Gravestone_-_geograph.org.uk_-_576196.jpg/250px-Carreg_Fedd_Ddwyieithog_-_Bilingual_Gravestone_-_geograph.org.uk_-_576196.jpg)

Studies of the linguistic landscape have been published from research done around the world. The field of study is relatively recent; "the linguistic landscapes paradigm has evolved rapidly and while it has a number of key names associated with it, it currently has no clear orthodoxy or theoretical core".[[7]](#cite_note-7) A special issue of the *[International Journal of Multilingualism](/w/index.php?title=International_Journal_of_Multilingualism&action=edit&redlink=1 "International Journal of Multilingualism (page does not exist)")* (3.1 in 2006) was devoted to the subject. Also, the journal *World Englishes* published a themed issue of five papers as a "Symposium on [World Englishes](/wiki/World_Englishes "World Englishes") and Linguistic Landscapes: Five Perspectives" (2012, vol. 31.1). Similarly, an entire issue of the *[International Journal of the Sociology of Language](/wiki/International_Journal_of_the_Sociology_of_Language "International Journal of the Sociology of Language")* (228 in 2014) was devoted to the subject, including looking at signs that show influences from one language on another language. In 2015 an academic journal devoted to this topic was launched, titled [*Linguistic Landscape: An International Journal*](https://benjamins.com/catalog/ll), from [John Benjamins](/wiki/John_Benjamins "John Benjamins"). There is also a series of academic conferences on the study of linguistic landscape.[[8]](#cite_note-8) A comprehensive, searchable Linguistic Landscape Bibliography[[9]](#cite_note-9) is available.[[10]](#cite_note-10) A 2016 special issue of Manusya (number 22, 2016)[[11]](#cite_note-11) begins with a history and summary of the field.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Because "the methodologies employed in the collection and categorisation of written signs is still controversial",[[13]](#cite_note-13) basic research questions are still being discussed, such as: "do small, hand-made signs count as much as large, commercially made signs?". The original technical scope of "linguistic landscape" involved plural languages, and almost all writers use it in that sense. Some scholars, however, use the term for public manifestations of writing in monolingual settings, e.g. in a German city,[[14]](#cite_note-14) or for controversies over spelling in the public sphere amid people's reactions, e.g. for English-language settings.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Multilingualism and monolingualism in signs

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/English-German_tombstone_in_Texas.jpg/250px-English-German_tombstone_in_Texas.jpg)

In much of the research the signs studied are [multilingual signs](/wiki/Bilingual_signs "Bilingual signs"), reflecting an expected multilingual readership. In other cases, there are monolingual signs in different languages, written in relevant languages found within a multilingual community.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Backhaus even points out that some signs are not meant to be understood so much as to appeal to readers via a more prestigious language (2007:58).

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b4/Pseudo-French_sign_in_American_French_restaurant.JPG)

Some signs are spelled to convey the aura of another language (sometimes genuinely spelled as in the other language, other times fictionally), but are still meant to be understood by monolinguals. For example, some signs in English are spelled in a way that conveys the aura of German or French, but are still meant to be understood by monolingual English speakers. Similarly, some signs use Latin script that is aestheticized to [look like](/wiki/Typeface#Mimicry_typefaces "Typeface") Chinese characters or Cyrillic script, in order to evoke the associated languages while still being readable to people who don't know them. For example, Leeman and Modan (2010) describe the use of aestheticized Latin script in the Washington DC's Chinatown and the Arab Quarter of Granada, Spain.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The study of linguistic landscape also examines such patterns as which languages are used for which types of institutions (e.g. country club, hospital, ethnic grocery store), which languages are used for more expensive/cheaper items (new cars or used cars), or which languages are used for more expensive/cheaper services (e.g. pool cleaning or washing machine repair). Also, the linguistic landscape can be studied across an area, to see which neighborhoods have signs in which languages. For example, Carr (2017) examined the languages of three cities in Southeast [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in her dissertation,[[19]](#cite_note-19) while Blommaert undertook an ethnography of his local neighbourhood in Antwerp, Belgium to examine how multilingual signs chronicles the complex social and cultural histories of a place.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The languages used in public signs indicate what languages are locally relevant, or give evidence of what languages are becoming locally relevant (Hult 2009; Kasanga 2012). In many multilingual countries, multilingual signs and packaging are taken for granted, especially as merchants try to attract as many customers as possible or people realize that they serve a multilingual community (Hult, 2014). In other places, it is a matter of law, as in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), where signs cannot be in English only, but must include French (Bill 101, *[Charte de la langue française](/wiki/Charte_de_la_langue_fran%C3%A7aise "Charte de la langue française")*). In Jerusalem it is legally required that 50% of a business's sign be in Hebrew.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), some signs are required to be in English and Spanish, such as warning signs about consuming [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") while pregnant.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/English-Amharic-Spanish_sign.jpg/250px-English-Amharic-Spanish_sign.jpg)

Linguistic landscape can also be applied to the study of competing scripts for a single language. For example, after the breakup of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), some signs in [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") were erected in the traditional Mongolian script, not just Cyrillic (Grivelet 2001). Similarly, in some Cherokee speaking communities, street signs and other public signage is written with the [Cherokee syllabary](/wiki/Cherokee_syllabary "Cherokee syllabary") (Bender 2008). Also, license plates in Greek Cyprus have been printed with Greek or Roman letters in different eras.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Different approaches to linguistic landscape studies

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Orthodox_bilingual_gravestone_in_Berwick_PA_1984.jpg/250px-Orthodox_bilingual_gravestone_in_Berwick_PA_1984.jpg)

More recently, scholars have rejected the purely quantitative approaches to Linguistic Landscape. For example, using Scollon and Scollon's (2003) framework of *geosemiotics,* researchers have analyzed the placement and relative size of different languages and signs.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Leeman and Modan (2009) proposed a "contextualized historical approach" to linguistic landscape that emphasizes the importance of considering how the signs came to be, and what they mean in a given context.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24) Their example of the different symbolic meanings of Chinese and English on Starbucks signs in Washington DC's Chinatown and a Shanghai shopping mall shows that it is unwise to draw conclusions based on the relative frequency of languages in signage.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)

The study of the linguistic landscape can also show evidence of the presence and roles of different languages through history.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Some early work on a specific form of linguistic landscape was done in cemeteries used by immigrant communities,[[27]](#cite_note-27) some languages being carved "long after the language ceased to be spoken" in the communities.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In addition to larger public signage, some who study linguistic landscapes are now including the study of other public objects with multilingual texts, such as banknotes in India which are labeled in over a dozen languages.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Politics and the linguistic landscape

While the politics of language, particularly around which particular languages are privileged and which are not, has been an important focus for the discipline since its earliest beginnings,[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) the role that the linguistic landscape can play in politics more generally is increasingly becoming a topic for investigation. Seargeant's study of *Political Activism in the Linguistic Landscape*,[[30]](#cite_note-30) for example, examines how the UK activist group [Led by Donkeys](/wiki/Led_By_Donkeys "Led By Donkeys") used a variety of sociolinguistic strategies drawing on the affordances of public space to expose acts of political hypocrisy. The book is also notable for using a graphic novel format to analyse its topic.

## Linguistic landscape studies on areas of conflict

The study of language in post-war and conflict-ridden areas has also attracted the interest of scholars who applied the Linguistic Landscape approach as a method to explore how language use in the public space represents ethnic groups, reflects territorial conflicts, expresses statehood and projects ideologies and socio-cultural identities. Themistocleous (2019) for instance explored the use of Greek and Turkish on public signs in the centre of Nicosia (Cyprus) and found that traditional discourses of separation and conflict are dominant in the public space but at the same time new discourses of unification, peace and integration slowly begin to surface.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Linguistic landscape in the Galician community

[Galicia or Galiza](/w/index.php?title=Galicia_or_Galiza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Galicia or Galiza (page does not exist)") is an officially autonomous community in Spain, where Galician and Spanish are spoken, although they do not peacefully coexist. Several scholars (e. g. Celso Alvarez Caccamo or Mario Herrero Valeiro) have studied the complexity of conflicts related to the coexistence of Galician and Spanish, and the power struggles around the standardization of Galician language, for example regarding orthography and pronunciation. In fact, despite the passing of laws to implement and enforce the usage of Galician in public discourse and public space, the very form of Galician chosen is at the center of political struggles, with the official variety (oficialista), which is more similar to Spanish, chosen over the one more similar to Portuguese (Reintegrationist). For this reason, different spellings of Galician appear in the Galician linguistic landscape, showing not only stratification of discourses, but also approptiation and contestation of public space and dialogicity in the linguistic landscape.

Although the legislation of the autonomous community of Galicia enforces the use of Galician in the public space, what is at stake is the form of Galician chosen for signs, banners, announcements, billboards, public road signs and toponyms, with the official form (oficialista) rejected by some activists and many (but not all) most radical Galician nationalists as too Spanishist, despite social and political elites’ efforts for constructing it so to look autonomous both from Spanish and Portuguese, and the more Portuguese one reclaimed as not only more appropriate from glottological perspective but also from a social and political perspective. This situation has been called “spelling war” by Mario Herrero Valeiro.
[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Linguistic landscape in the Valencian community

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/09/Bilingual_road_sign_in_Valencian_Community.jpg)

[Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia") is an officially bilingual city, where Valencian and Spanish populations coexist. In this territory the [Valencian language](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language") lives in a situation of [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), and therefore the Valencian public institutions must maintain, protect and promote the use of the Valencian language. To ensure this, in 2005 the Valencia City Council developed "the Reglamento municipal sobre uso y normalización del valenciano en el municipio de Valencia" ("Municipal Regulations on the Use and Standardisation of Valencian Language in the Municipality of Valencia"[[33]](#cite_note-33)). The autonomous legislation of Valencia considers the use of Valencian as the preferred language for signs, banners, announcements, billboards, public road signs and toponyms. Moreover, the autonomous government and the municipal regulations from Valencia encourage private entities to use Valencian over Spanish in their efforts to raise the prestige and recognition of the language, which, despite being official, is used by minority throughout the territory.

Socially speaking, [the Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community") is a territory with a great number of inhabitants who are either monolingual in Spanish, or bilingual in Spanish-Valencian, and it is a strongly touristy region. There is international tourism, especially from [England](/wiki/England "England") and Northern Europe, and also national tourism. Among the use of other languages within the territory, the use of English is noteworthy as a lingua franca or vehicular language. For this reason, many shops use English in order to be accessible to a wider public. As described by Bruyèl-Olmedo and Juan-Garau, "among the number of languages featured on signs, shop fronts, billboards and the like, English enjoys a privileged position when it comes to addressing a multilingual, heterogeneous readership" [[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Basque and Spanish languages

[The Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") has invested in the protection of bilingualism, with particular regard to the introduction of measures aimed at ensuring the normalisation of the [Basque language](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") in institutions and the achievement of equal rights in its use and enjoyment for citizens, which at the moment is not fully achieved, especially in the working environment. In fact, according to the Unesco Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger, Basque is a minority and vulnerable language in its own territory, and is in an asymmetrical situation with respect to the other official language, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"). The challenge, therefore, is to bring the two official languages ([Castilian](/wiki/Castilian_Spanish "Castilian Spanish") and [Euskara](/wiki/Euskara "Euskara")) up to the same level in practice, beyond the formal recognition they have already enjoyed since 1978, the year in which the newborn Constitution established in the second paragraph of Article 3 that all other languages should be equally official in their respective Autonomous Communities.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Bookstore_with_two_signs_in_Bilbao.jpg/250px-Bookstore_with_two_signs_in_Bilbao.jpg)

This historic concession constituted a decisive change of direction after the years of [Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco")'s dictatorship, during which the regime had banned the use of the Basque language and attempted to erase its history and traditions.

The Statute of Autonomy of the Basque Country (1979), for its part, proclaimed the status of the [Basque language](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") as its own official language in the Autonomous Community of [the Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") and the right of all persons to know and use both official languages (Article 6.1). Finally, Article 6.4 provided that the Royal Academy of the Basque Language-Euskaltzaindia is the official consultative institution with regard to Basque.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Getting to the present day, in 2009 of the approximately two million people living in the Autonomous Community of the Basque Country, or Euskadi, only around 35% spoke Euskera, the [Basque language](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"), a non-Indo-European language isolate. Apart from that, notably Euskera had also a 9% of speakers in the Autonomous Community of [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre") and 26% in the Basque territories in the south of [France](/wiki/France "France").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

At the time, it was in the second year of the Basque Government's fourth planning period (2008-2012). Since then, the major intervention made is that contained in Decree 179/2019 of 19 November 2019, which gave to each local council the decision-making power on how to organise the use of both languages in its internal and public relations.

This decree put an end to the stage of a single rule for all local entities, and opened a new time in which each local institution could decide which language to use according to its sociolinguistic reality.

Among its objectives: to make Basque the language of work and of relations between administrations, to rationalise the use of translations and interpretations and to promote the use of the language in and from municipalities.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Examples

![The three-language (Tamil, English and Hindi) name board at the Tirusulam railway station in South India. Almost all railway stations in India have signs in three or more languages (English, Hindi and the local language).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Trisulam_railway_station_nameboard.JPG/120px-Trisulam_railway_station_nameboard.JPG)
![Spanish-English sign at Cathedral Santuario de Guadalupe in Dallas, Texas; the congregation has both English and Spanish speakers.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Bilingual_sign_at_Dallas_Cathedral.JPG/120px-Bilingual_sign_at_Dallas_Cathedral.JPG)

Finnish and four tourist languages — English, German, French, and Russian — on the right.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Nine_languages_at_Ivalo%27s_S-market.jpg/120px-Nine_languages_at_Ivalo%27s_S-market.jpg)

![Sign in Roman script but Hebrew words, a hostel in Haifa, Israel catering to European gentiles](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/%D7%91%D7%99%D7%AA_%D7%A7%D7%A8%D7%A1%D7%A0%D7%98%D7%99_-_%D7%91%D7%AA_%D7%92%D7%9C%D7%99%D7%9D_%281%29.JPG/120px-%D7%91%D7%99%D7%AA_%D7%A7%D7%A8%D7%A1%D7%A0%D7%98%D7%99_-_%D7%91%D7%AA_%D7%92%D7%9C%D7%99%D7%9D_%281%29.JPG)
![This Spanish sign was advertising a mobile home for rent in a largely Hispanic neighborhood in Texas. Capturing urban language
------------------------

Menu

[Home](https://murbll.wordpress.com/)What are Linguisitic Landscapes?

What are Linguisitic Landscapes?
================================

_Sign, sign, everywhere a sign,_

_Blockin’ out the scenery, breaking my mind._

_Do this. Don’t do that._

_Can’t you read the sign?_

(‘Signs’ – Five Man Electrical Band, 1970)

No matter where you are in the world today, language is everywhere you look. It’s used in shop signs, products in the supermarket, the names of buildings, menus, graffiti, airports, public transport, shopping centres, notices, advertising posters and hoardings.

**What are Linguistic Landscapes?**

The study of the linguistic landscape is a relatively new area, which draws from several academic disciplines such as applied linguistics, sociolinguistics, anthropology, sociology, psychology and cultural geography (Ben-Rafael, Shohamy and Barni, 2010). The term _Linguistic_ _Landscape_ was first used by Landry and Bourhis in a paper published in 1997, when they defined it as;

_The language of public road signs, advertising billboards, street names, place names, commercial shop signs, and public signs on government buildings combine to form the linguistic landscape of a given territory, region or urban agglomeration._

Landry and Bourhis, 1997

Shohamy and Gorter have since widened the scope of the definition to include:

_…language in the environment, words and images displayed and exposed in public spaces, that is the center of attention in this rapidly growing area referred to as_ Linguistic Landscape _(LL)._

(Shohamy and Gorter, 2008)

In another study, Dagenais, Moore, Sabatier, Lamarre and Armand (2008) introduce the idea of the Linguistic Landscape as ‘environmental print’, i.e. cities as ‘texts’.

**Features of the linguistic landscape**

Most studies of the Linguistic Landscape are socio-economic in nature, i.e. they seek to find correlations between the use of certain languages (such as English) in parts of a city and compare them to the general standard of living in those areas. There is general agreement that language use in the linguistic landscape falls into one of two categories, _top-down_ (public signs, created by the state and local government bodies) and _bottom-up_ language use (created by shop owners, private businesses, etc.), as is summarised in the table below:

[![Image 1: TD BU](https://murbll.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/td-bu.jpg?w=274&h=300)](https://murbll.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/td-bu.jpg)

Ben-Rafael, Shohamy, Amara and Trumper-Hecht, 2006

However, I prefer to think of it more as a cline, with varying degrees of how ‘official’ and ‘unofficial’ the language use is, as below:

[![Image 2: cline](https://murbll.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/cline.jpg?w=300&h=213)](https://murbll.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/cline.jpg)

This allows us to include things which are more complex to define, such a T-shirts, which are designed and manufactured by a company, but in which the individual expresses a personal choice by wearing. Their appearance in the urban landscape therefore has both top-down and bottom-up influences.

**Why is the linguistic landscape important?**

_LL, indeed, constitutes the very scene – made of streets, corners, circuses, parks, buildings – where society’s public life takes place. As such, this scene carries crucial sociosymbolic importance as it actually identifies – and thus serves as the emblem of societies, communities and regions._

Ben-Rafael, Shohamy, Amara and Trumper-Hecht, 2006

The linguistic landscape is important not only because it provides the backdrop to our day-to-day lives, but also as a valuable language learning resource. It shapes how we interact as a society and gives us our identity. And the best thing about it is that it’s everywhere, open to everyone and free.

**References & further reading**

This is introduction is part of [a longer article aimed at language teachers](http://issuu.com/britishcouncilportugal/docs/inenglish-digital-08) which I wrote for In-English Digital, a free online publication.

Gorter, D (Ed) _Linguistic Landscape: A New Approach to Multilingualism_ Multilingual Matters 2006

Landry, R and Bourhis, RY _Linguistic landscape and ethnolinguistic vitality_ Journal of Language and Social Psychology 16 (1), 23-49

Shohamy, E, Ben-Rafael, E and Barni, M (eds) _Linguistic Landscape in the City_ Multilingual Matters 2010

Shohamy, E and Gorter, D (eds) _Linguistic Landscape: Expanding the Scenery_ Routledge, 2009

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/linguisticlandscapeforlanguagestudents-columbia29mar2016-160330181651/95/linguistic-landscape-for-language-study-5-638.jpg?cb=1459394074
DESCRIPTION: A slide presents the older definition of linguistic landscape as the language used on public road signs, advertisements, billboards, street and place names, and government signs, emphasizing how these elements form the linguistic landscape of a territory, with a citation from Landry & Bourhis (1997).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Analyzing signs reveals cultural meanings, social hierarchies, and linguistic visibility. The placement and language used reflect community identities and power relations.
Understanding the cultural background and social dynamics surrounding signs enhances interpretation. Contextual factors influence language choices and the representation of different communities.
Signs reflect and shape community identities, often highlighting power dynamics between dominant and marginalized groups. Language use in signage can assert or challenge social power.
Signage serves as a visual representation of community diversity. Different languages and symbols indicate the presence and influence of various cultural groups within urban spaces.
